course_description:
This course is an intermediate level elective on the structure and rationale of parliamentary democracy and elections. It offers a detailed examination of the regulation of political participants, finances and electoral processes. The course will be taught in a theoretical and contextual manner, with particular attention to values such as political equality and competitiveness. It seeks to develop an understanding of the pivotal but under-appreciated role of legal norms in shaping representative democracy.

Law of Political Institutions ﾠis of inherent interest, given the basic importance of parliaments to our laws and governance, and of electoral democracy to our public life. It covers two broad areas:

There may be guest presenters across the course. These will be advised in week 1 and via course announcements. (In previous years they have included a former Speaker and current MPs or local political figures).

Classes. Attendance at the weekly class is expected. Attending, prepared to discuss the set questions, is important to mastering or doing well in this course.

The class is timetabled each Monday, 4-7pm. It will be in two parts. A 'lecture' of 70 minutes or so, followed by a 10 minute break. Then a 'seminar' where students seated in groups will address the set questions for the week, with your lecturer guiding and summarising discussion of those questions, followed by a short quiz (not for marks). The plan is to finish each class around 6:30pm. Classes will be recorded (audio + slides/visuals). But while that kind of recording will capture the 'lecture' half of each class, it will be much less useful for the 'seminar' discussion.

Before each class, a brief ‘Preview’ video will be available, foreshadowing the key themes for that class. This is to help students prepare for attending or listening to the lecture.

The course was last offered in 2023. There have been modest changes to the course coverage, readings, assignment and style of delivery. There are 12 classes, with none on Labour Day (week 10).

Background . If you are on exchange from overseas - or haven't taken much notice of electoral democracy in Australia - this public guide is very useful, especially the first 5, short, chapters: Hot Topics: Voting and Elections (2025).

course_aims:
Education Mission Statement

The mission of the School of Law is to educate you in and about the law. We seek to assist you to think critically about the law and to investigate its structures, principles, policies, and values. This is achieved by engaging with you as fellow investigators in a range of critical and intellectual conversations about the law and its place in society. Our role is thus to guide you in your own intellectual journey in the law and to equip you with the necessary skills that will enable you to continue that journey long after you have left the School.

Course Aims

The course aims to introduce students to key issues in institutional design, regulation and the role of the courts across two related domains:

learning_outcomes.description: Explain, and research more deeply, the important features of the regulation of parliament, elections and political activity.
Structure and argue essay, seminar discussion and exam problems, applying both practical and theoretical considerations relevant to the course.
Understand the role that law and institutions play in regulating representative democracy.
Integrate the institutions and rules studied into their political and normative contexts: those contexts are important to an understanding of - and potential reform in - this field.

assessment_summary.title: Research Assignment
assessment_summary.due_date: Research Method Statement 15/04/2026 2:00 pm; Research Assignment 22/04/2026 2:00 pm

assessment_details.title: Research Assignment
assessment_details.due_date: Research Method Statement 15/04/2026 2:00 pm Research Assignment 22/04/2026 2:00 pm
assessment_details.task_description: This short written assignment lets students demonstrate their research and writing skills, whilst deepening their understanding of issues from the first half of the course. That is, from weeks 1-6: eg parliamentary law, the composition or distribution of parliament, representation, political participation under the constitution, or distinctive features of the franchise. Guidelines will be released on the course website on Monday week 3. The final assignment is due Wednesday week 8. (Given the mid-semester break, that is over 6 weeks to complete it). One week prior to the due date (ie Wednesday of week 7) you must submit a one page statement of Research Method. This will be be further explained in the guidelines and class. It is worth 4 marks or 10% of the total for the assignment. It will be marked as: 'acceptable' = 4 marks, 'on time but inadequate' = 2 marks, or 'not submitted' = 0 marks. The guidelines will include a choice of set topics. Students may frame an alternative topic, but only after consultation with Graeme Orr as course coordinator. Such a bespoke topic must relate to issues or themes from the first half of the course. Overseas exchange students are encouraged to frame a comparative law topic (eg compare Australia and their home country on a particular issue). The word limit for the assignment itself is strict: no words in excess of 2200 will be marked. This limit excludes citation detail in footnotes . But such notes must not contain discursive material, eg commentary . This word limit is separate from the Research Method statement. Given the word limit, consider a sharp focus for your essay. This may mean choosing a narrow question or aspect of a larger topic. That said, do not just jump into a seemingly 'narrow' question, without reading and thinking 'around' the topic and its context, before framing your assignment in a focused way. The assignment is to be submitted as a Word document, via a link on the course website. Late submission without a law school extension will be penalised at the usual rate. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools in this assignment is permitted. But must be disclosed as part of your Research Method statement. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct . Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Assignments must be submitted as Word files, via the submission link on the course website. There will be a separate link to upload your Research Method statement.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 7 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Extension to Assessment Due Date
The maximum extension for an assessment item due within the semester / teaching period in which the course is offered is 7 days. In exceptional circumstances, an extension may be approved for a further 7 days. Where a student is incapacitated for a period longer than 14 days, they are advised to apply for removal of course.
A student may apply for an extension to assessment due date if they are unable to meet an assessment deadline due to extenuating circumstances. Please refer to the Applying for an extension page on my.UQ for further details on acceptable reasons for an extension and for instructions on how to apply . Additional information on extensions can be found within the Assessment Procedure .
Spoken or written notification of difficulties with assessment deadlines to a course coordinator or the School does not constitute an authorised extension.
A request for an extension to an assessment due date must be submitted through my.UQ as soon as it becomes evident that an extension is needed, but no later than one calendar day after the assessment task submission due date and time . Requests for extensions received after the application due date and time, must include evidence of the reason for the late request, and will require the decision maker listed in the Decision-Maker Tables in the Student Grievance Resolution Procedure to determine whether to accept the request for consideration.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
10% penalty applies to every day or part day late. This applies to the 4 marks available for the Research Method statement, and to the Assignment itself.
Penalties for Late Submission
The following penalties apply to late submission of an assessment item, assuming that an extension has not been approved, and that late submission is academically possible (e.g., it does not delay feedback to a cohort, or interfere with course delivery).
First 7 periods of 24 hours (or part thereof) - 10% per 24 hours of the maximum possible mark for the assessment item. For example, a report worth a maximum of 40 marks, submitted 28 hours late will attract a penalty of 8 marks, calculated as 2 periods of 24 hours x 10% x 40 marks.
More than 7 periods of 24 hours - 100%.

assessment_details.title: End-of-semester Examination (Open Book)
assessment_details.other_conditions: Time limited, Secure. See the conditions definitions
assessment_details.task_description: The exam may consist of: 1. a normative essay linking themes in the course with a broad area of regulation, 2. a problem solving/advice question, and 3. one or more shorter questions requiring critical evaluation of some specific case's or statutory regime. Students need to keep up with classes and readings over the course. We will discuss the format, coverage and expectations for the examination in the last two classes. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct . Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.exam_details: Planning time 10 minutes Duration 120 minutes Calculator options No calculators permitted Open/closed book Open book examination - any written or printed material is permitted; material may be annotated Exam platform Paper based Invigilation Invigilated in person

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Weekly Schedule of Topics and Classes There are 12 classes, over 13 weeks. Each with a study guide of its own. Including holiday breaks, and noting assessment dates these are: Week 1. The law of politics. This introduces the course, the evolution of parliaments and theories about the 'purpose' of the law of politics. Week 2. Parliament: institution and structure . This covers the structure and composition of parliaments within ideas of representation, and the context of federalism and bicameralism. Week 3. Parliamentary law and privileges . Parliaments guard their privileges, including the absolute privilege of parliamentary speech and discipline of MPs and others for contempt. The Research Assignment guidelines are released Monday of week 3. Week 4. Political participation and the Constitution. Courts recognise an implied freedom of political communication and access to the franchise: but not any broader 'right' to political participation, non-discrimination or equality. Week 5. Mapping parliaments and weighting votes . This considers the mapping of parliaments and weighting of votes involved in 'redistributions' of the seats in lower houses. Week 6. Who can vote . This explores both the definition of the franchise, and compulsory voting rules in Australia. Week 7. Qualifications to be elected. This focuses on candidacies: who is qualified to be an MP and restrictions on being listed on the ballot paper. The Research Method statement and the Research Assignment are due on Wednesday of week 7 and week 8, respectively. There is no class Monday 6 April due to Easter and the mid-semester break between weeks 7 and 8. Week 8. Political parties under the law . This examines how the law impacts (or enables) the internal affairs of parties within the idea of freedom of association. Week 9. Regulation of campaigning and misinformation . This considers the (limited) legal norms constraining either the form or content of electoral speech. There is no class in week 10 due to Monday 4 May being Labour Day holiday. Week 11. The courts' role in electoral politics . This covers judicial involvement in disputed elections or administration. Week 12. Money in politics. This focuses on the new system for national elections and participants, and the new South Australian model banning donations to parliamentary parties/MPs. Week 13. Referendums and direct democracy + Exam overview . This briefly considers how and why referendums or plebiscites are held. Plus exam coverage and a past exam question. Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L04
